original_abstract: It is widely accepted that training deep neural networks successfully requires datasets with thousands of annotated samples. In this paper, we propose a novel network architecture and training strategy that leverages aggressive data augmentation to utilize available annotated samples more efficiently. The architecture features a symmetric design consisting of a contracting path to capture global context and an expanding path that enables precise localization. We demonstrate that this network can be trained end-to-end using very few images, outperforming the previous state-of-the-art—a sliding-window convolutional network—on the ISBI challenge for segmenting neuronal structures in electron microscopy stacks. Using the same architecture trained on transmitted light microscopy images (phase contrast and DIC), we won the 2015 ISBI cell tracking challenge in these categories by a significant margin. Furthermore, the network is highly efficient, segmenting a 512x512 image in less than one second on a modern GPU. Our full Caffe-based implementation and the pre-trained networks are available at http://lmb.informatik.uni-freiburg.de/people/ronneber/u-net.
humanized_abstract: Training deep neural networks typically requires a large amount of labelled training data, such as thousands of images. To make the most out of the limited training data and still being able to train and apply the network fast, we show a method that makes efficient use of few images by employing a highly aggressive data augmentation approach. We then present a new architecture consisting of a contracting path for image feature extraction and an expanding path for localisation. Our network can then be learnt end-to-end using very few images and is even able to beat the state-of-the-art solution that makes use of the well known approach of sliding-window convolutional neural networks for the ISBI 2012 Segmentation Challenge for neuronal reconstruction from EM stacks. We were able to win the 2015 ISBI cell tracking challenge for phase contrast and DIC transmitted light microscopy images with the same basic architecture after having shown its strength on images from a bright field microscope. Moreover, the network is able to make segmentations of images of the size 512x512 within one second on a GPU. Our Caffe code and pre-trained models can be downloaded from http://lmb.informatik.uni-freiburg.de/people/ronneber/u-net.

Moreover, the network is able to make segmentations of images of the size 512x512 within one second on a GPU. Our Caffe code and pre-trained models can be downloaded from http://lmb.informatik.uni-freiburg.de/people/ronneber/u-net.
